quiz.question: What is the standard fuel capacity of the Piper Cherokee PA-28-140 when filled to the bottom of the filler neck indicator?

answerOptions.rationale: Filling to the indicator at the bottom of the filler neck establishes the standard fuel load for this aircraft model.
This figure represents the total reserve fuel capacity rather than the standard quantity indicated by the filler neck.
This is the individual capacity of a single fuel tank, not the total standard quantity for the entire aircraft.
While plausible for similar aircraft, this value is not supported by the specific specification features of the PA-28-140.

quiz.hint: Refer to the standard quantity specified for the fuel system in Section II.

quiz.question: Which airspeed provides the best rate of climb ($V_y$) for the PA-28-140 at gross weight?

answerOptions.rationale: This speed is used for the best angle of climb, which is intended for clearing obstacles in a short distance rather than gaining altitude quickly over time.
This specific speed is designated to achieve the maximum gain in altitude over a given period of time at sea level.
This speed is recommended for enroute climbs to improve visibility and cooling, but it does not provide the maximum rate of vertical ascent.
This speed is the maximum at which flaps can be extended and is too high for an efficient climb.

quiz.hint: This speed is higher than the best angle of climb but lower than the enroute climb speed.

quiz.question: What is the minimum octane fuel required for the Lycoming O-320-E2A engine?

answerOptions.rationale: While this is an approved higher grade, it is not the minimum grade required for the engine's 7:1 compression ratio.
This is the recommended alternative when the primary minimum grade is unavailable, but it is not the actual minimum design requirement.
The engine is designed for a minimum of $80/87$ octane, which is identified by its distinct red color.
This high-grade military fuel exceeds the requirements for this low-compression general aviation engine.

quiz.hint: Look for the fuel grade associated with the color red.

quiz.question: During the pre-takeoff ground check, what is the maximum allowable differential drop between magnetos at $2000$ RPM?

answerOptions.rationale: This is the maximum total drop allowed for a single magneto, not the difference between the two.
The difference in RPM drop between the left and right magnetos must stay within this limit to ensure balanced ignition performance.
A difference of this magnitude would indicate a significant disparity in the health of the two independent ignition systems.
While a lower drop is better, the manual allows for a slightly larger margin before the system is considered out of tolerance.

quiz.hint: This value represents the allowed variation between the two ignition sources.

quiz.question: At what engine speed (RPM) should the magneto check be performed?

answerOptions.rationale: This is the idle warm-up speed and is insufficient for checking the ignition system under load.
This speed is used for warming up the engine or clearing it after using alternate fuels, but not for the formal ground check.
This specific power setting provides the necessary engine load to accurately measure magneto performance and vacuum system levels.
This is the rated speed for full power at lower weights, which is too high for a safe static ground check.

quiz.hint: Check the Ground Check instructions in Section III.

quiz.question: What is the proper tire pressure for both the main and nose wheels of the Cherokee 140?

answerOptions.rationale: This pressure is too high and could lead to excessive tire wear or a harsh ride on the landing gear.
Maintaining $24$ pounds of pressure in all three tires ensures proper shock absorption and even tread wear.
Under-inflation can cause the tires to overheat or slip on the wheel rim during braking.
This value exceeds the manufacturer's specification for all landing gear tires on this aircraft.

quiz.hint: All three landing gear tires use the same specified pressure.

quiz.question: According to the handbook, when should the electric fuel pump be turned 'ON'?

answerOptions.rationale: The electric pump serves as a safety backup to the engine-driven pump during the most critical phases of flight.
While used for starting, it must also be operational during take-off and landing to ensure fuel pressure consistency.

answerOptions.text: Only when the main tank is empty
answerOptions.rationale: The pump is a system redundancy and should be used regardless of the fuel quantity in the tanks during specific flight phases.
The manual specifies pump usage based on flight phase (take-off and landing) rather than altitude.

quiz.hint: This pump is used as a safety precaution during high-workload phases near the ground.

quiz.question: What is the maximum oil capacity of the Lycoming O-320-E2A engine?

answerOptions.rationale: This is the minimum safe quantity required for operation, not the maximum capacity of the sump.
This is a common operating level, but it does not represent the full capacity defined in the specifications.
The oil sump is designed to hold a maximum of $8$ quarts for engine lubrication and cooling.
This exceeds the design capacity of the engine's oil system as stated in the Power Plant section.

quiz.hint: Refer to the specification features under Power Plant or the preflight check list.

quiz.question: The visual stall warning light is designed to activate at what speed relative to the actual stall?

answerOptions.text: At the exact moment of the stall
answerOptions.rationale: A warning that occurs exactly at the stall provides no time for the pilot to take corrective action.
This margin provides the pilot with a timely alert to increase airspeed or reduce the angle of attack before the aircraft actually stalls.
This would result in premature warnings during normal slow-flight approaches and landings.

answerOptions.text: Only when flaps are fully extended
answerOptions.rationale: The stall warning system functions independently of flap position to provide safety throughout the flight envelope.

quiz.hint: The system is designed to provide a small buffer of airspeed to allow for pilot reaction.

quiz.question: What is the purpose of the anti-servo tab on the Cherokee's stabilator?

answerOptions.text: To increase the maximum speed of the aircraft
answerOptions.rationale: The tab is a control surface enhancement and does not contribute to increasing the aircraft's top speed.

answerOptions.text: To provide longitudinal trim and extra stability
answerOptions.rationale: The tab moves in the same direction as the stabilator, providing both feel to the pilot and a means to trim the aircraft for hands-off flight.

answerOptions.text: To act as a primary air brake
answerOptions.rationale: Longitudinal control and trimming are its primary functions; it is not designed to create significant drag for slowing down.
Turning radius is primarily affected by bank angle and airspeed, not the pitch trim system.

quiz.hint: Think about the role of the horizontal tail in flight stability and control 'feel'.

quiz.question: Where is the battery located in the Piper Cherokee 140?

answerOptions.rationale: The firewall contains the brake reservoir and fuel strainer, but the battery is located further aft for weight and balance reasons.
The space under the seats is occupied by the spar carry-through and control mechanisms.
The battery is housed in a dedicated, vented container under the baggage floor to keep it protected and accessible for maintenance.

answerOptions.text: In the tail cone near the stabilator
answerOptions.rationale: While the serial number plate is near the stabilator, placing a heavy battery there would adversely affect the center of gravity.

quiz.hint: Access for inspection is obtained by raising a hinged floor panel in the storage area.

quiz.question: What is the recommended procedure for starting a cold engine (below $40^{\circ}F$)?

answerOptions.text: Open the throttle fully and engage the starter
answerOptions.rationale: Opening the throttle fully could lead to a dangerous overspeed immediately after the engine starts.

answerOptions.text: Prime the engine with $1$ to $3$ full strokes of the priming pump
answerOptions.rationale: In cold temperatures, extra fuel is needed in the cylinders to facilitate combustion because fuel does not vaporize as easily.

answerOptions.text: Use the electric fuel pump for $5$ minutes before starting
answerOptions.rationale: Running the pump for an extended period without starting could flood the system or drain the battery.

answerOptions.text: Avoid using the primer and only use throttle pumps
answerOptions.rationale: Throttle pumping is only recommended for temperatures above $40^{\circ}F$; colder weather requires the more direct fuel delivery of the primer.

quiz.hint: Cold weather requires a manual injection of fuel into the engine intake.

quiz.question: In the event of a suspected engine overprime, what is the correct corrective action?

answerOptions.text: Continue priming until the engine fires
answerOptions.rationale: This would worsen the condition by adding even more excess fuel to the cylinders.

answerOptions.text: Open the throttle fully and rotate the engine with the starter
answerOptions.rationale: Opening the throttle allows maximum air to enter the cylinders, helping to clear out the excess fuel while the engine is turned over.

answerOptions.text: Wait for $30$ minutes for the fuel to evaporate
answerOptions.rationale: While waiting might eventually work, the manual provides a specific mechanical procedure to clear the engine immediately.

answerOptions.text: Turn on the carburetor heat during the next start attempt
answerOptions.rationale: Carburetor heat is not used for starting and would not help clear excess fuel from the cylinders.

quiz.hint: The goal is to get as much air as possible through the engine to clear out excess fuel.

quiz.question: What is the maximum speed at which the flaps can be extended ($V_{fe}$)?

answerOptions.rationale: This is the climb and approach speed, well below the structural limit for flap extension.
This is the enroute climb speed, but the aircraft is designed to handle flap loads at slightly higher speeds.
Extension above this speed could cause structural damage to the flaps or the actuating linkage.
This is the top speed of the aircraft, which is far beyond the safe operating range for extended flaps.

quiz.hint: Check the Approach and Landing section in Section III.

quiz.question: Which document governs if there is an inconsistency with the Owner's Handbook?

answerOptions.rationale: The engine manual only covers the powerplant, not the entire aircraft's operation.

answerOptions.text: The Airplane Flight Manual (AFM) approved by the F.A.A.
answerOptions.rationale: The AFM is the legally binding document for aircraft operation and always takes precedence over the handbook.
This guide is for maintenance reference and does not supersede operational flight manuals.
Advisory Circulars provide guidance but are not specific to the legal limitations of a particular aircraft's flight manual.

quiz.hint: This is mentioned in the 'Notice' at the very beginning of the handbook.

quiz.question: What is the standard wing span of the Cherokee 140?

answerOptions.rationale: This figure actually represents the length of the aircraft from nose to tail.
The aircraft is designed with a constant $30$-foot wing span across both weight categories.
This is the wheel tread (distance between the main wheels), not the wingspan.
This represents the height of the aircraft to the top of the tail.

quiz.hint: Look in the Dimensions table in Section I.

quiz.question: When checking the fuel system during preflight, what must be 'ON' to properly drain the lines through the gascolator?

answerOptions.text: The Master Switch and Electric Fuel Pump
answerOptions.rationale: The electric pump is required to push fuel from the selected tank through the lines to the strainer for an effective check.
The ignition should always be OFF during preflight for safety to prevent accidental engine starts.
Carburetor heat is a mechanical ducting system and has no role in the fuel draining process.
Cabin heating is unrelated to the fuel system's preflight drainage procedures.

quiz.hint: One electrical component is needed to pressurize the fuel system for the drain check.

quiz.question: What is the recommended interval for changing the engine oil under normal conditions?

answerOptions.rationale: While conservative, the manual standard for an engine without a full-flow filter is higher than this.
This is the standard recommended frequency to ensure engine longevity and remove contaminants.
This interval is only acceptable if the engine is equipped with a full-flow cartridge type oil filter.

answerOptions.text: Once per year regardless of hours
answerOptions.rationale: Maintenance is primarily based on operating hours rather than a simple calendar duration in this handbook.

quiz.hint: Check the Oil Requirements in Section V.

quiz.question: Which flight control surface is used as a step for cabin entry and must be fully retracted to support weight?

answerOptions.rationale: Ailerons are located further out on the wing and are not structurally designed or positioned to be used as a step.
The flap incorporates a lock in the up position specifically to allow it to function as a boarding step on the right side.
The horizontal tail is located at the rear and is not part of the cabin entry path.
The rudder is a vertical surface and cannot support the weight of a person entering the cabin.

quiz.hint: This surface has three extended positions but must be 'UP' for stepping.

quiz.question: What is the maximum baggage capacity for a Cherokee 140 licensed at a gross weight of $2150$ lbs?

answerOptions.rationale: This is the limit for the lower $1950$ lb gross weight category.
The higher gross weight model allows for double the baggage weight of the original version, provided other seating limits aren't exceeded.
This higher limit only applies if the specific family seat and safety belts are installed.
This is below the allowable limit for any version of the Cherokee 140.

quiz.hint: Look for the asterisk notation in the Baggage specifications in Section I.

quiz.question: When stopping the engine, why should the mixture be pulled to 'Idle Cut-Off' rather than just turning off the magnetos?

answerOptions.text: To prevent the battery from draining
answerOptions.rationale: The battery is controlled by the master switch, not the engine's fuel mixture.

answerOptions.text: To ensure no fuel remains in the cylinders to cause 'after-firing'
answerOptions.rationale: Cutting the fuel at the source ensures the engine stops completely and prevents unintended ignition of residual vapors.

answerOptions.text: To lubricate the cylinders for the next start
answerOptions.rationale: Cutting the fuel does not provide lubrication; lubrication comes from the oil system which stops when the engine stops.

answerOptions.text: To clear the radio of static interference
answerOptions.rationale: Engine shutdown procedures are for the powerplant's safety and do not affect radio static.

quiz.hint: Think about how the engine is deprived of its 'food' to stop its operation.

quiz.question: What is the normal extension of the main landing gear oleo struts under static load?

answerOptions.rationale: This is the specified extension for the nose gear, which is smaller than the main gear struts.
The main gear requires this specific amount of exposed piston to provide adequate shock absorption.
This extension would indicate an over-inflated strut, resulting in a very stiff landing gear.
This would indicate a low-pressure or leaking strut that needs service.

quiz.hint: The main gear extension is more than an inch longer than the nose gear extension.

quiz.question: In the Piper 'Full Time Power' electrical system, what does the ammeter indicate?

answerOptions.text: The total voltage of the battery
answerOptions.rationale: Voltage is measured by a voltmeter; an ammeter specifically measures the flow of current in amperes.

answerOptions.text: The load in amperes placed on the system
answerOptions.rationale: Unlike older systems that only show battery discharge, this system shows the actual current being used by the aircraft's equipment.

answerOptions.text: The remaining life of the alternator
answerOptions.rationale: The ammeter shows current flow, but it cannot predict the mechanical or electrical lifespan of the alternator.

answerOptions.text: The temperature of the electrical wires
answerOptions.rationale: There is no gauge in the standard Cherokee 140 for monitoring electrical wiring temperature.

quiz.hint: Think about what 'load' means in the context of electrical current.

quiz.question: What should be done if oil pressure is not indicated within $30$ seconds of starting the engine?

answerOptions.text: Increase the RPM to $2000$ to prime the pump
answerOptions.rationale: Increasing RPM without oil pressure could cause catastrophic engine damage due to lack of lubrication.

answerOptions.text: Stop the engine and determine the trouble
answerOptions.rationale: A lack of oil pressure indication is a critical warning that the engine is not being lubricated, requiring an immediate shutdown.

answerOptions.text: Wait for another minute for the oil to warm up
answerOptions.rationale: The $30$-second window is the maximum safe time allowed; waiting longer risks permanent engine failure.

answerOptions.text: Tap the gauge to see if it is stuck
answerOptions.rationale: While a gauge might stick, the safe operational procedure is to assume the engine has no pressure until proven otherwise.

quiz.hint: This is a critical safety step to prevent engine damage.

quiz.question: Which control should be avoided during prolonged ground operations to prevent intake of unfiltered air?

answerOptions.rationale: The throttle must be used for taxiing and cannot be avoided.
The mixture is essential for engine operation and does not affect air filtration.
When carburetor heat is 'ON', air bypasses the filter, which can allow dust and grit into the engine, especially on unimproved fields.
Cabin vents provide air to the occupants, not the engine, and do not affect engine longevity.

quiz.hint: This control provides heated air that bypasses the high-efficiency dry-type filter.

quiz.question: What is the recommended enroute climb speed to improve visibility and cooling?

answerOptions.rationale: This is the best rate of climb speed, but the lower nose attitude of a higher speed is preferred once away from the airport.
Climbing at $100$ mph provides a better forward view over the nose and increased airflow to cool the engine.
This speed results in a very high nose attitude, which significantly reduces visibility.
This is a cruising speed and would result in a very poor rate of climb.

quiz.hint: Check the Climb section in Section III.

quiz.question: How should the aircraft be laterally leveled for weighing purposes?

answerOptions.text: By deflating one of the main tires
answerOptions.rationale: Tire deflation is used for longitudinal (front-to-back) leveling, not lateral (side-to-side) leveling.

answerOptions.text: By placing a level across the baggage compartment floor along the rear bulkhead
answerOptions.rationale: This specific structural point is designated as the reference for determining if the aircraft is level from wingtip to wingtip.

answerOptions.text: By checking the fuel levels in both tanks
answerOptions.rationale: Fuel levels affect weight but are not used as a leveling reference point.

answerOptions.text: By aligning the wingtips with the horizon
answerOptions.rationale: Visual alignment with the horizon is not precise enough for the technical requirements of weighing or rigging.

quiz.hint: This involves using a level inside the storage area behind the seats.

quiz.question: What is the glide distance of a PA-28-140 starting from $10,000$ feet above the terrain?

answerOptions.rationale: The aircraft is much more efficient than this; $5$ miles would be an extremely steep descent.
Based on the Glide Distance chart, the aircraft covers about $1.5$ miles for every $1000$ feet of altitude.
This exceeds the performance capability of the aircraft under standard glide conditions.
The glide ratio allows for more distance than a $1:1$ mile-per-thousand-feet relationship.

quiz.hint: Consult the Glide Distance vs Altitude chart in Section IV.

quiz.question: To maintain best lateral trim during flight, how should fuel be managed?

answerOptions.text: Keep both tank selectors on 'Both' at all times
answerOptions.rationale: The PA-28-140 fuel system does not have a 'Both' setting; tanks must be selected individually.

answerOptions.text: Use one tank for an hour, then switch to the other until nearly exhausted
answerOptions.rationale: This method ensures the weight of the fuel is distributed as evenly as possible across the wings throughout the flight.

answerOptions.text: Always use the left tank first because of engine torque
answerOptions.rationale: The manual does not specify a 'left first' rule, but rather a timed rotation between tanks.

answerOptions.text: Only switch tanks when the engine begins to cough
answerOptions.rationale: Waiting for engine symptoms is dangerous and poor fuel management practice.

quiz.hint: Look at the Cruising instructions in Section III regarding lateral trim.

quiz.question: What is the specific model of the propeller used on the Cherokee 140?

answerOptions.rationale: The Cherokee 140 uses a fixed-pitch propeller, not a constant-speed model.
This fixed-pitch aluminum alloy unit is the standard propeller for all performance figures in the handbook.
Three-blade propellers are typically found on higher-performance aircraft, not the trainer-class Cherokee 140.
The handbook specifies an aluminum alloy construction for the propeller.

quiz.hint: Refer to the Engine and Propeller description in Section II.
